title: Detecting copy number variants
abstract: This application relates to diagnostic methods based on calling copy number variants. A computer processor receives whole genome sequencing data for a patient and identifies candidate regions in the whole genome sequencing data for potential copy number variations. The processor also receives amplification data generated for the patient at the candidate regions. The amplification data indicates binding of oligonucleotide probes to the candidate regions in the patient. Each of the oligonucleotide probes is specific to one of the candidate regions. The processor can then compare the amplification data at with reference amplification data. The reference amplification data indicates binding of the reference oligonucleotide probes to the reference regions having a known number of copies. Finally, the processor confirms the presence or absence of a CNV at the candidate regions based on the comparison.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS: 1. A method for identifying a copy number variation, the method comprising: (i) receiving whole genome sequencing data for a patient; (ii) identifying multiple candidate regions in the whole genome sequencing data for potential copy number variations; (iii) receiving amplification data generated for the patient at the multiple candidate regions, said amplification data indicating binding of one or more oligonucleotide probes to one or more of the candidate regions in the patient, each of the one or more oligonucleotide probes being specific to one of the multiple candidate regions; (iv) comparing the amplification data at (iii) with reference amplification data, said reference amplification data indicating binding of one or more reference oligonucleotide probes to one or more reference regions having a known number of copies; and (v) confirming the presence or absence of a CNV at the multiple candidate regions based on the comparison at (iv). 2. The method of claim 1, wherein: (i) if the amplification data at one of the candidate regions indicates a quantity of copy number different to the known number of copies for the one or more reference regions, the CNV is confirmed at the candidate region; and/or (ii) if the amplification data at one of the candidate regions indicates a quantity of copy number that is the same as the number of copies for the one or more reference regions, the CNV is rejected at the candidate region. 3. The method of claim 1 or 2, comprising performing one or more DNA amplification assays on a sample obtained from the patient to generate the amplification data and/or the reference amplification data. 4. The method of any one of the preceding claims, comprising designing one or more oligonucleotide probes specific for each of the multiple candidate regions 

 identified, wherein each oligonucleotide probe is designed to be complementary to a unique sequence within the candidate region for which it is specific. 5. The method of claim 4, wherein designing each of the one or more oligonucleotide probes comprises obtaining a sequence from a reference genome at one of the multiple candidate regions and using the sequence from the reference genome in that oligonucleotide probe. 6. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the amplification data is data from quantitative polymerase chain reaction (QPCR) or digital PCR. 7. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein each of the one or more oligonucleotide probes comprises a first end and a second end and the binding of the oligonucleotide probe to the one or more of the candidate regions in the patient comprises ligation between the first end and the second end. 8. The method of claim 7, wherein ligation between the first end and the second end enables amplification of that probe. 9. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the amplification data is data from one or more multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) assays. 10. The method of claim 9, wherein the MLPA assay results in amplicons having a unique length for each candidate region. 11. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein one or more of the multiple candidate regions has a length of less than 10,000 bases. 12. The method of claim 11, wherein one or more of the multiple candidate regions has a length of less than 5,000 bases. 13. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the reference regions are regions in the genome of the patient. 14. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein identifying the multiple candidate regions comprises determining a coverage value and identifying the multiple candidate regions where the coverage value is less than the average coverage value of other regions. 15. The method of claim 14, wherein identifying the multiple candidate regions comprises determining break points in split reads and/or discordant pairs to confirm candidate regions identified based on the coverage value. 16. The method of any one of the preceding claims, wherein identifying the multiple candidate regions comprises determining break points in split reads and/or discordant pairs and identifying the multiple candidate regions where the paired reads are discordant or split in comparison to the reference region. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein identifying the multiple candidate regions comprises determining the coverage value to confirm candidate regions identified based on the split read and/or discordant pair evidence. 18. The method of any one of the preceding claims, further comprising determining a copy number at one or more of the multiple candidate regions. 19. The method of any one of the preceding claims, further comprising filtering the multiple candidate regions based on disease causation after step (ii) and before step (111) . 20. Software that, when executed by a computer, causes the computer to perform the method according to any one of the preceding claims. 21. A computer system for identifying a copy number variation, the computer system comprising a processor configured to (i) receive whole genome sequencing data for a patient; (ii) identify multiple candidate regions in the whole genome sequencing data for potential copy number variations; (iii) receive amplification data generated for the patient at the multiple candidate regions, said amplification data indicating binding of one or more oligonucleotide probes to one or more of the candidate regions in the patient, each of the one or more oligonucleotide probes being specific to one of the multiple candidate regions; (iv) compare the amplification data at (iii) with reference amplification data, said reference amplification data indicating binding of one or more reference oligonucleotide probes to one or more reference regions having a known number of copies; and (v) confirm the presence or absence of a CNV at the multiple candidate regions based on the comparison at (iv).
